text: its back. Its power cell flared in overload, and its thrashing ceased . He swept his optics over the area, detecting no further motion apart from that caused by the wind. If the reavers had any other companions, they’d decided the fight was too risky. He looked down to assess the damage he’d taken. His casing had deflected three of the bullets, but three more had penetrated. A diagnostic check indicated no significant damage to any of his systems . Ronin slung his rifle over his shoulder and checked the fallen bots for anything of value. Their rifles were pieced together from ancient parts — just like everything was, these days — but kept in such poor condition that he was surprised they had fired at all. He doubted they’d hold together over the short trip back to town . The ammunition looked reliable enough. He put the fourteen rounds into a coat pocket. His rifle wasn’t chambered for their caliber, but they’d have value in Cheyenne. Other than that, the bots had nothing worth taking. The undamaged parts in their casings would sell for good credit in Cheyenne or any other town, but Ronin left them be. It was illogical to think so, but something about pilfering a deactivated bot for pieces seemed wrong . He followed the skeletal, mostly-buried remains of automobiles back to the road. Cracked asphalt was still visible through the dirt, which was ever-moving thanks to the constant wind. More scrubby grass grew through the gaps. Soon, he left the old four-lane freeway and its dead vehicles behind, turning onto a side road leading directly to Cheyenne. Locals called it Camp Road, though its name had once been longer and less relevant . Here on the edge of the Dust, any path to shelter was one worth walking . In the eight months he’d spent based out of Cheyenne, Ronin had never encountered reavers so close to the town . He halted when he turned to look at the massive building to the north. Its walls had been battered by at least two hundred years of harsh weather, wearing away the paint and scratching the metal beneath. Twisted steel beams jutted from the partially-collapsed roof like the ribs of a decomposing pronghorn. All around the structure lay dozens of trailers — two hundred and fourteen of them, exactly. Thirty-seven were still hitched to the ancient machines that had once dragged them from one corner of the land to another . Decades of windstorms had deposited mounds of sediment around the building and the trailers alike. There was little left to salvage from the place. Ronin had searched each trailer at least twice, and gone through the accessible portions of the building four times. It was exactly fifteen hundred feet from end to end . Despite his thoroughness, he occasionally discovered small objects that held some value . He turned his optics to the west, waiting a moment for them to adjust to the glare of the evening sun. Five miles on, Cheyenne’s Bot District stood largely untouched by the ravages of time, quiet beneath the heat-shimmering air. Even at an easy pace, he could be back within an hour, surrounded by a sound roof and four sturdy walls instead of hundreds of miles of nothingness . Cheyenne wouldn’t go anywhere. It had stood since before the Black Out, before his circuits were scrambled and the world was undone. With or without Ronin, the bots and humans calling it home would continue their lives. And the sky, though perpetually gray and hazy, bore no storm sign . There was time enough for another brief search . He walked toward the ruined building, dirt crunching beneath his boots. Stopping at one of the battered doors, he grasped the latch with a bare-metal hand — the synthetic skin had long since been claimed by the Dust — and pulled. The door swung open with little resistance, groaning on its hinges . The building was dark and quiet inside. Most of the remaining materials were too large or impractical to remove — bundles of long, precisely cut wood boards now gray with age, sheets of crumbling dry wall, miles and miles of pipe and wire, and heavy sacks of dust that hardened when wet. Cement, his memory said, though the writing on the bags had long since faded beyond reading . Sometimes, there were heavy tread marks in the dirt outside. Ronin guessed that Warlord, the leader of Cheyenne, had a heavy-lifter or hauler of some sort to drag the large materials back to town when they were needed for repairs. Easy enough, with the right tools and relative nearness. But most of the bots in Cheyenne had been sheltered, bearing little understanding of how to scavenge, how to pull treasures from the Dust . Ronin adjusted the lay of his pack and swung his rifle into his hands. His thumb brushed over the smooth spot on the grip, worn over the fifty-seven years, four months, and twelve days it had been in his possession. He stepped forward slowly to keep his scrap from clanging, optics adjusting to compensate for the limited light . A distant, damaged memory told him this was a reception area, but he could not recall what its true purpose had been . Shafts of light pierced the ceiling in spots, illuminating floating motes of dust. The stuff was everywhere, in everything. That was reason enough to return to Cheyenne; he needed repairs before his insides filled with grit. That would, in time, finish the job the bullets had begun . After a quick search — most everything was in the same condition it had been the last time he was here, down to the undisturbed dirt on the floor — he moved around the broad, broken desk directly ahead of the entrance and through the door marked Associates Only . As he walked down the long hallway, the building’s walls groaned in the wind.
